questions.question: What is the 'critical path' in a quantum circuit?

options.text: The path with the most CNOT gates
The sequence of operations that determines the total circuit depth
The path between the first and last qubit
The most error-prone sequence of gates

questions.explanation: The critical path is the longest chain of dependent operations in the circuit. It determines the total depth because all other operations can be parallelized while waiting for the critical path to complete.

questions.question: Why is reducing two-qubit gate count more important than single-qubit gate count for NISQ devices?

options.text: Two-qubit gates take longer to execute
Two-qubit gates have 10-100x higher error rates
Two-qubit gates require more classical control
Two-qubit gates use more qubits

questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates (like CNOT) typically have error rates 10-100x higher than single-qubit gates on current hardware. For example, single-qubit errors ~0.1% vs two-qubit errors ~1%. Minimizing two-qubit gates has the largest impact on overall fidelity.

questions.question: What typically happens during transpilation routing?

options.text: Gates are decomposed into basis gates only
SWAP gates are inserted to match hardware connectivity
Measurement operations are optimized
Classical registers are allocated

questions.explanation: During routing, the transpiler inserts SWAP gates to move qubit states so that two-qubit gates can be executed between physically connected qubits. Each SWAP typically decomposes to 3 CNOT gates, significantly increasing circuit depth.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of higher transpilation optimization levels (e.g., level 3)?

options.text: Faster compilation time
Better gate cancellation and circuit simplification
More readable circuit output
Compatibility with more backends

questions.explanation: Higher optimization levels apply more aggressive circuit simplification passes, including gate cancellation, commutation analysis, and resynthesis. This typically results in shorter circuits at the cost of longer compilation time.

questions.question: How many CNOT gates does a SWAP gate typically decompose into?

options.text: 1 CNOT
2 CNOTs
3 CNOTs
4 CNOTs

questions.explanation: A SWAP gate decomposes into 3 CNOT gates: SWAP = CNOT(0,1) · CNOT(1,0) · CNOT(0,1). This is why routing overhead can significantly increase CNOT count and circuit depth.

questions.question: Circuit depth is always equal to the total number of gates in the circuit.
questions.explanation: False. Circuit depth counts the number of time steps (layers), not total gates. Gates on different qubits can execute in parallel in the same layer. A circuit with 10 gates might have depth 3 if 7 gates execute in parallel.

questions.question: What is a common sign that transpilation routing is causing problems?

options.text: The circuit runs faster than expected
CNOT count increases significantly after transpilation
Single-qubit gates are removed
Measurements appear at the beginning of the circuit

questions.explanation: A significant increase in CNOT count after transpilation indicates routing overhead. If your circuit goes from 10 CNOTs to 40 CNOTs, the transpiler likely inserted many SWAP gates (each = 3 CNOTs) to satisfy hardware connectivity constraints.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid strategies to reduce circuit depth?

options.text: Parallelize independent gates
Use circuit identities to cancel redundant gates
Choose better initial qubit layout
Increase the number of measurement shots

questions.explanation: Parallelizing independent gates, canceling redundant gates (like CNOT·CNOT = I), and optimizing initial layout all reduce depth. Increasing shots only reduces statistical error but doesn't change circuit depth or noise accumulation per shot.
